comment_text.sn24.2:1.1: Most of the views mentioned in this Saṁyutta are discussed elsewhere, so I will give references for the relevant notes.
comment_text.sn24.2:1.3: This is the basic expression of the self doctrine (attavāda) as at SN 22.8:1.5.

root_text.sn24.2:1.2: “Kismiṁ nu kho, bhikkhave, sati, kiṁ upādāya, kiṁ abhinivissa evaṁ diṭṭhi uppajjati:
root_text.sn24.2:1.5: “rūpe kho, bhikkhave, sati, rūpaṁ upādāya, rūpaṁ abhinivissa evaṁ diṭṭhi uppajjati:
root_text.sn24.2:1.10: viññāṇe sati, viññāṇaṁ upādāya, viññāṇaṁ abhinivissa evaṁ diṭṭhi uppajjati:
root_text.sn24.2:2.9: api nu taṁ anupādāya evaṁ diṭṭhi uppajjeyya:
root_text.sn24.2:2.12: “Yampidaṁ diṭṭhaṁ sutaṁ mutaṁ viññātaṁ pattaṁ pariyesitaṁ anuvicaritaṁ manasā tampi niccaṁ vā aniccaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn24.2:2.14: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vā taṁ sukhaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn24.2:2.16: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vipariṇāmadhammaṁ, api nu taṁ anupādāya evaṁ diṭṭhi uppajjeyya:
root_text.sn24.2:3.1: “Yato kho, bhikkhave, ariyasāvakassa imesu ca ṭhānesu kaṅkhā pahīnā hoti, dukkhepissa kaṅkhā pahīnā hoti …pe… dukkhanirodhagāminiyā paṭipadāyapissa kaṅkhā pahīnā hoti—
root_text.sn24.2:3.2: ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako sotāpanno avinipātadhammo niyato sambodhiparāyano”ti.

translation_text.sn24.2:1.2: “Mendicants, when what exists, because of grasping what and insisting on what, does the view arise:
translation_text.sn24.2:1.3: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
translation_text.sn24.2:1.4: “Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. …”
translation_text.sn24.2:1.5: “When form exists, because of grasping form and insisting on form, the view arises:
translation_text.sn24.2:1.6: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’
translation_text.sn24.2:1.10: consciousness exists, because of grasping consciousness and insisting on consciousness, the view arises:
translation_text.sn24.2:1.11: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’
translation_text.sn24.2:2.12: “That which is seen, heard, thought, known, attained, sought, and explored by the mind: is that permanent or impermanent?”
translation_text.sn24.2:2.14: “But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?”
translation_text.sn24.2:2.16: “But by not grasping what’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, would the view arise:
translation_text.sn24.2:2.17: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
translation_text.sn24.2:3.1: “When a noble disciple has given up doubt regarding these six grounds, and has given up doubt in suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation,
translation_text.sn24.2:3.2: they’re called a noble disciple who is a stream-enterer, not liable to be reborn in the underworld, destined for awakening.”
